channel.title: Comments for page "I Forget Myself in This Wondrous Night"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "I Forget Myself in This Wondrous Night"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much!! ♥️ I know you didn't post it back when you first drew it, but this is still technically the first fanart I've ever gotten. I love your interpretation of 1467 here and his fading away. Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Back when this was first released, I read it, and I loved it so much that I created fanart for it. I've cleaned it up and posted it here.
I wasn't an artist, and didn't know Deci. But now I sometimes am, and I do. Here's for you, Deci.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got here from your AmA, and I wasn't disappointed. I read it before reading the SCP it was based on, and I actually found it very harrowing that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is still the best thing I’ve ever written. A lot went into this one. I spent a lot of time writing this but it still falters a bit, with some slightly-too-long sentences and a bit of (needed) contrivance to make things happen the way I wanted.

I’ve always wanted to explore this sort of thing with other characters and dig into their characters in this sort of style, but it’s a pretty hard style to keep doing. But nonetheless! A very good one.

item.title: Re: +55
item.content:encoded:
That makes sense, thanks for explaining.

And congrats on your article's success!

what was going on during the containment breach at the start of the story?

Truth be told, this was the only part of the story I didn't think through. I wanted an impetus for the rest of the story to unfold. If 1467 was wanting to escape before to see his family or at least figure out a sense of individuality (and those were hints I picked up on when reading the SCP), it was more plausible for me to have it happen during a containment breach than figure out a contrived way of making him escape on his own. It also sets up the scale at which he starts to fade; still there for the most part but rapidly deteriorating, versus already gone enough to slip out without the Foundation noticing.

If there'd been a breach, what were those people doing having lunch in the cafeteria? Had they locked themselves in there because of the breach? If so, why did they unlock the door for Smith?

Yeah, they locked themselves in the there due to the breach. My take on it is a bit of a headcanon. If he's already being lost due to his ability, what people perceive he says and does might be different than what he actually says and does. They might hear someone banging on the door and the word "Smith" and possibly think it's other personnel, or a personnel named "Smith" and go to open it. I also tried to write it with the intention that the breach was either dying down or with minimal risk to justify an employee just opening the door there for someone else.

Thank you very much <3

EDIT: Also, this is now a year old and at +55. I'm incredibly proud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was beautiful.

I can certainly see why you're proud of this one, Decibelle. Having read this and a few of your other works, it's clear you have a gift for character studies - for making us sympathize with your characters by making us feel what they feel. Your portrayal of Mr. Smith is especially impressive when you consider how much of a non-entity (pun intended) he was in the original article. Also, I thought that bittersweet, cathartic ending was lovely, and your title is easily one of the most evocative I've seen on the site (and it only gets more beautiful when you get to the end and realize what it actually means).

I really don't have any nitpicks or criticisms with this one, but I do have a small question: what was going on during the containment breach at the start of the story? If there'd been a breach, what were those people doing having lunch in the cafeteria? Had they locked themselves in there because of the breach? If so, why did they unlock the door for Smith? (I know this has no bearing on the story, but I'm just curious.)

+10

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having been more acquainted with Shadowrun now, I get what you mean, and the similarities are good! Albeit from different circumstances, but still!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this. You are truly master of disjointed imagery. Skipping from moment to moment, only able to notice when he knows he's real.

This reminds me of something from a Shadowrun sourcebook. On the subject of Cybermancy, you are able to cram a ridiculous amount of cyberware into someone, enough to kill a normal individual. Technically the body dies during the process. They then bind the spirit into the body. Problem is, if the subject's mind wanders for too long, the spirit will go where they normally go, leaving behind a very expensive pile of meat. So they implant a stimulator into the memory centers of the brain, giving them a "jolt" every time the cyberzombie starts to vegetate.

"Smith" is in kind of a similar circumstance. If he doesn't remind himself he exists, he doesn't.

+1 for subtle existential horror crawling beneath the surface.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you like them! Really, my big goal is to subject everyone on the site to my music taste, hehe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for this, and for introducing me to Penguin Cafe Orchestra…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you!

Also, nope. "He", in this case, is 1467.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent portrayal of 1467. You might wanna check your first footnote, though, it refers to a woman as "he".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! It was a tough ordeal trying to give him character and life on his own, and I'm glad it paid off for you and many others :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woah, nice writing! I really really like this one, nice job with Smith! c: I like most the character you gave him, it worked really well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome tale that made me feel sad for "Mr. Smith." Great job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you liked it! I really have to thank you for helping set me on the path I went with for this tale. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perfect. Liked it as a draft. Like it now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All the footnotes represent his thoughts and thinking, yes. If he's talking with someone or next to someone after all, he can try to analyze them and compare them against himself so he can reaffirm himself and how he looks to continue to keep existing. It's not foolproof, mind you, and it's conjecture on my part for how he thinks (given his character is barely expanded upon in the SCP article), but I tried.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am not entirely sure that I understand what the footnotes are doing. Representing his thoughts as they fade?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! :D I wouldn't have been able to push on and continue it without your support :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful.

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded:
Its anomalous condition continues to deteriorate. By current estimates, the Foundation will have lost it by 20██-██

Before I edit this further; I listened to an album called Music From the Penguin Club Orchestra exclusively when writing this. Give it a listen. It's great. The song The Sound Of Someone You Love Who's Going Away And It Doesn't Matter, in particular, helped a ton to set the mood. Give it a go!

Okay, so this is a tale about SCP-1467, which was featured last. Thanks to thedeadlymoose, TwistedGears, and Scantron for reading the first two sections when I originally wrote it and showed it to them. Without their approval and encouragement, I would not have continued to write this.

Huge thanks to Cimmerian and qntm for informing where to go with this and how to improve it such. I didn't take all of their suggestions, or even that much, but they helped me make it better, I believe.

Thanks to TwistedGears, Dmatix, and snorlison for proofreading as well and correcting stuff. Also thanks to thedeadlymoose, who'll inevitably rip me apart for whatever poor grammar or structure I have XD

Finally, thanks to everyone in #site19 who read it and said they liked it!

+ I admittedly have a secret ambition with this tale.
- I admittedly have a secret ambition with this tale.

I want to at least get half of the votes for this tale that 1467 does, cause I'm ambitious like that.

- I admittedly have a secret ambition with this tale.
+ Ported from my author page discussion thread.
– hide block

This is obstinately a tale about SCP-1467. And it wholly is, but it's a lot more.

I published this May 7, 2015. In truth, it was being written for years. This is where my fascination with writing about mental health is at its most obvious. The reason why I connect and love 1467 so much is because it's real easy to read it as a metaphor for… a lot. It's up to reader interpretation, I suppose. I'll spare you how I view it; what's important is the writing. I wrote this a week or two after I broke up with my girlfriend in April. The first part of the tale was all I wrote, and it was done in a hurry one night. thedeadlymoose, djkaktus, Communism will win, and TwistedGears all liked it and encouraged me to continue, so I did. Over the course of three nights (one of those being an all-nighter), I wrote and wrote and wrote and edited this like you could not believe. It shows; only someone who's mad would write an article that essentially treats its audience like their therapist.

I love prose that's off-kilter, purple and normal, grounded in reality but ever so fanciful. It immediately represents a viewpoint that comes from two different places at once, even if it shares a common origin (in this case, the third-person view of Mr. Smith), which I think helps establish the mood I'm going for. It's, in essence, a story about being lost and losing yourself. It's a feeling most can relate to, but especially people with mental illness like myself, so I had to make it as universal as I could. And I did I think. I pulled it off real well.

The only reason why this doesn't surpass 3090 for me is because Smith isn't an original character. I adapted a character with gaps in his story and then constructed a personal narrative around. This is the challenge inherent to this site in writing tales about characters in other tales or SCPs. This is a story, by the way, that could have only been written and published in a certain period of my life. It was either then or never. I'll never write a story like this again, nor would I want to. It's the manifestation of emotions and expressions I only could have gotten out then. But if I were totally normal and still happily dating, would it be worth not having this story? Yes, it would.
